---
title: "Special Occasions and Drinking Associations"
format: html
---

# Special Occasions and Drinking Associations

Many people associate alcohol with celebration, relief, or connection. Over time, the brain links these experiences with drinking, which can make certain events feel risky—even when you are committed to sobriety. This is why occasions like birthdays, holidays, paydays, and reunions often require extra preparation.

Recovery does NOT mean avoiding enjoyable experiences. It means creating new rituals so that meaningful moments stay enjoyable without alcohol. With repetition, these new patterns become natural.

High-risk situations can also involve group influence, where others’ behavior increases cravings. The most effective strategy is to plan **before** the event—when thinking is clear and emotions are lower.

::: callout
## How to Use This Worksheet

Before each upcoming event:

1.  Identify past patterns\
2.  Plan a specific sober alternative\
3.  Decide how you will respond if urges increase\
4.  Set boundaries and exit strategies in advance\
:::

## Occasion Planning Sheet

| Upcoming occasion | Old alcohol pattern | New sober ritual (specific & enjoyable) | Support plan (who/what helps) | Exit plan (when/how you’ll leave) | Backup plan if urge rises |
|------------|------------|------------|------------|------------|------------|
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Example

-   **Old pattern:** Drinking at holiday parties\
-   **New ritual:** Bring a favorite non-alcoholic drink and stay engaged in conversations\
-   **Support plan:** Text sponsor before and after\
-   **Exit plan:** Leave after 90 minutes\
-   **Backup plan:** Step outside, call someone, or leave immediately

------------------------------------------------------------------------

![](https://github.com/jjcurtin/aud_support/blob/main/modules/images/holiday.png?raw=true){fig-align="center"}

::: callout
## High-Risk Awareness

Watch for these situations during events:

-   Feeling pressured to participate\
-   Romanticizing past drinking experiences\
-   Sudden increases in stress or emotion\
-   Losing track of time or personal boundaries

If you notice these, **use your plan immediately**.
:::
